name: Kenny Clarke
abstract: Kenneth Clarke Spearman (January 9, 1914 – January 26, 1985), known professionally as Kenny Clarke and nicknamed Klook, was an American jazz drummer and bandleader. A major innovator of the bebop style of drumming, he pioneered the use of the ride cymbal to keep time rather than the hi-hat, along with the use of the bass drum for irregul…

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Kenny Clarke with one of my -- he was on my first recording session, Here’s Comes Frank Foster, done in 1954. I played with him on a couple of other subsequent recordings.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Barefield
about_quotes.evidence: I was in the house group. You know, there was me and Thelonious Monk and Nick Fenton and Harold West and Klock sometimes, Kenny Clark.

about_quotes.speaker: James Moody
about_quotes.evidence: When I joined Dizzy’s band in ‘46 it was I think, Monk was the piano player, Thelonious Monk, and Kenny Clarke was the drummer
about_quotes.doc_title: James Moody interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 13, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Katz
about_quotes.evidence: I was in the house rhythm section at the Café Bohemia with Oscar Pettiford and Kenny Clarke.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Katz interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Mickey Roker
about_quotes.evidence: I fell in love with Kenny Clarke... the guy that I listened to in my developing stages
about_quotes.doc_title: Mickey Roker interviewed by Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Annie Ross
about_quotes.evidence: By that time Kenny Clarke and I were hooked up... Klook and I were living in Fort Apache, the Bronx... Klook and Moody had the band... I learned it upside down and backwards... Klook said, 'Okay, get out If You Could See Me Now.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Annie Ross — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
